name: halmos

metadata.summary: A symbolic testing tool for EVM smart contracts

documentation:
# Halmos

[![PyPI - Version](https://img.shields.io/pypi/v/halmos)](https://pypi.org/project/halmos)
[![Python Version from PEP 621 TOML](https://img.shields.io/python/required-version-toml?tomlFilePath=https%3A%2F%2Fraw.githubusercontent.com%2Fa16z%2Fhalmos%2Frefs%2Fheads%2Fmain%2Fpyproject.toml)](https://github.com/a16z/halmos)
[![License](https://img.shields.io/github/license/a16z/halmos)](https://github.com/a16z/halmos/blob/main/LICENSE)
[![chat](https://img.shields.io/badge/chat-telegram-blue)](https://t.me/+4UhzHduai3MzZmUx)

[**Install**](https://github.com/a16z/halmos?tab=readme-ov-file#installation)
| [**Getting Started**](https://github.com/a16z/halmos/blob/main/docs/getting-started.md)
| [**Examples**](https://github.com/a16z/halmos/blob/main/examples/README.md)
| [**FAQ**](https://github.com/a16z/halmos/wiki/FAQ)
| [**Chat**][chat]
| [**awesome-halmos**](https://github.com/redtrama/awesome-halmos)

Halmos is a _symbolic testing_ tool for EVM smart contracts. A Solidity/Foundry frontend is currently offered by default, with plans to provide support for other languages, such as Vyper and Huff, in the future.

You can read more in our post: "_[Symbolic testing with Halmos: Leveraging existing tests for formal verification][post]_."

Join the [Halmos Telegram Group][chat] for any inquiries or further discussions.

[post]: <https://a16zcrypto.com/symbolic-testing-with-halmos-leveraging-existing-tests-for-formal-verification/>
[chat]: <https://t.me/+4UhzHduai3MzZmUx>

## Installation

### ⭐ Using `uv` (recommended for most users)

```sh
# install uv if you don't have it already
curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh

# install the latest version of halmos for the current user and add it to PATH
uv tool install halmos

# or, install the development version from the repository
# uv tool install git+https://github.com/a16z/halmos

# after installing, you can update halmos to the latest version with:
uv tool upgrade halmos
```

### Using `docker`

You can download a pre-built Docker image that contains python, halmos, its dependencies, foundry, solvers, etc.:

```sh
docker pull ghcr.io/a16z/halmos:latest
```

### Using `pip` (for advanced users)

Note: this is not recommended because of the extra work required to manage the python version and the virtual environment. But if you know what you are doing, and need the extra control, you can do it like this:

```sh
# make sure you have a suitable python version installed, e.g.:
python3.12 --version

# create and activate a virtual environment with an explicit python version
python3.12 -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate

# install the latest version of halmos
pip install halmos

# or, install the development version from the repository
pip install git+https://github.com/a16z/halmos
```

## Usage

```sh
cd /path/to/src
halmos
```

For more details:

```sh
halmos --help
```

Alternatively, you can run the latest halmos Docker image available at [ghcr.io/a16z/halmos](ghcr.io/a16z/halmos):

```sh
cd /path/to/src

# mount '.' under /workspace in the container
docker run -v .:/workspace ghcr.io/a16z/halmos:latest
```

## Getting Started

Refer to the [getting started guide](docs/getting-started.md) and the [examples](examples/README.md) directory.

## Contributing / Developing

Refer to the [contributing guidelines](CONTRIBUTING.md), and explore the list of issues labeled ["good first issue" or "help wanted."][issues]

[issues]: <https://github.com/a16z/halmos/issues?q=is%3Aopen+is%3Aissue+label%3A%22good+first+issue%22%2C%22help+wanted%22>

## Disclaimer

_These smart contracts and code are being provided as is. No guarantee, representation or warranty is being made, express or implied, as to the safety or correctness of the user interface or the smart contracts and code. They have not been audited and as such there can be no assurance they will work as intended, and users may experience delays, failures, errors, omissions or loss of transmitted information. THE SMART CONTRACTS AND CODE CONTAINED HEREIN ARE FURNISHED AS IS, WHERE IS, WITH ALL FAULTS AND WITHOUT WARRANTY OF ANY KIND, EXPRESS OR IMPLIED, INCLUDING ANY WARRANTY OF MERCHANTABILITY, NON-INFRINGEMENT OR FITNESS FOR ANY PARTICULAR PURPOSE. Further, use of any of these smart contracts and code may be restricted or prohibited under applicable law, including securities laws, and it is therefore strongly advised for you to contact a reputable attorney in any jurisdiction where these smart contracts and code may be accessible for any questions or concerns with respect thereto. Further, no information provided in this repo should be construed as investment advice or legal advice for any particular facts or circumstances, and is not meant to replace competent counsel. a16z is not liable for any use of the foregoing, and users should proceed with caution and use at their own risk. See a16z.com/disclosures for more info._

modules.name: halmos.exceptions
documentation:
Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM) Exceptions
========================================

Exceptions thrown during EVM execution.

Note: this is a modified version execution-specs' src/ethereum/<fork>/vm/exceptions.pyd

classes.name: halmos.exceptions.PathEndingException
documentation:
Base class for any exception that should stop the current path exploration.

Stopping path exploration means stopping not only the current EVM context but also its parent contexts if any.

classes.name: halmos.exceptions.HalmosException
documentation:
Base class for unexpected internal errors happening during a test run.
Inherits from RunEndingException because it should stop further path exploration.

classes.name: halmos.exceptions.InfeasiblePath
documentation: Raise when the current path condition turns out to be infeasible.

classes.name: halmos.exceptions.FailCheatcode
documentation:
Raised when invoking DSTest's fail() pseudo-cheatcode.
Inherits from RunEndingException because it should stop further path exploration.

classes.name: halmos.exceptions.EvmException
documentation: Base class for all EVM exceptions.

classes.name: halmos.exceptions.Revert
documentation:
Raised by the `REVERT` opcode.

Unlike other EVM exceptions this does not result in the consumption of all gas.

classes.name: halmos.exceptions.ExceptionalHalt
documentation:
Indicates that the EVM has experienced an exceptional halt. This causes
execution to immediately end with all gas being consumed.

classes.name: halmos.exceptions.StackUnderflowError
documentation: Occurs when a pop is executed on an empty stack.

classes.name: halmos.exceptions.StackOverflowError
documentation: Occurs when a push is executed on a stack at max capacity.

classes.name: halmos.exceptions.OutOfGasError
documentation:
Occurs when an operation costs more than the amount of gas left in the
frame.

classes.name: halmos.exceptions.InvalidOpcode
documentation: Raised when an invalid opcode is encountered.

classes.name: halmos.exceptions.InvalidJumpDestError
documentation:
Occurs when the destination of a jump operation doesn't meet any of the
following criteria:

  * The jump destination is less than the length of the code.
  * The jump destination should have the `JUMPDEST` opcode (0x5B).
  * The jump destination shouldn't be part of the data corresponding to
    `PUSH-N` opcodes.

classes.name: halmos.exceptions.MessageDepthLimitError
documentation: Raised when the message depth is greater than `1024`

classes.name: halmos.exceptions.WriteInStaticContext
documentation:
Raised when an attempt is made to modify the state while operating inside
of a STATICCALL context.

classes.name: halmos.exceptions.OutOfBoundsRead
documentation:
Raised when an attempt was made to read data beyond the
boundaries of the buffer.

classes.name: halmos.exceptions.InvalidParameter
documentation: Raised when invalid parameters are passed.

classes.name: halmos.exceptions.InvalidContractPrefix
documentation: Raised when the new contract code starts with 0xEF.

classes.name: halmos.exceptions.AddressCollision
documentation: Raised when trying to deploy into a non-empty address

functions.name: halmos.calldata.parse_type

Construct a function signature string from the given function abi item.

See `tests/test_cli.py:test_str_abi()` for examples.

If no mapping exists, construct it using the raw ABI list.

documentation: number of bytes

class_variables.name: halmos.calldata.EncodingResult.static

methods.name: halmos.calldata.Calldata.get_dyn_sizes
documentation:
Return the list of size candidates for the given dynamic parameter.

The candidates are derived from --array_lengths if provided; otherwise, default values are used.

methods.name: halmos.calldata.Calldata.create
documentation:
Create calldata of the given function.

Returns:
    A tuple containing the generated calldata, and the size information
    for all dynamic parameters included in the calldata.

methods.name: halmos.calldata.Calldata.encode
documentation:
Create symbolic ABI encoded calldata

See https://docs.soliditylang.org/en/latest/abi-spec.html

For dynamically-sized parameters, multiple size candidates are considered.
A generalized encoding is used to represent these candidates, where the size field is symbolized,
and the elements are provided assuming the maximum size of the candidates.
The size symbols are mapped to their size candidates in the Path, and paths are later split based on these candidate values.
(See sevm.calldataload for more details.)

Note that this encoding approach leverages the non-uniqueness property of ABI encoding.
In other words, the generalized encoding is not optimized for the shortest size when smaller sizes are used.

methods.name: halmos.mapper.BuildOut.get_by_name
documentation:
Return the build output json for the given contract name.

Raise a HalmosException if the contract is not found, or cannot be uniquely determined.

The optional filename argument is required, if the contract name appears in multiple files.

documentation: Extracts the function signature (first 4 bytes) from calldata

functions.name: halmos.utils.unbox_int
documentation: Converts int-like objects to int, returns x otherwise

functions.name: halmos.utils.int_of
documentation: Converts int-like objects to int or raises NotConcreteError

functions.name: halmos.utils.match_dynamic_array_overflow_condition
documentation:
Check if `cond` matches the following pattern:
    Not(ULE(f_sha3_N(slot), offset + f_sha3_N(slot))), where offset < 2**64

This condition is satisfied when a dynamic array at `slot` exceeds the storage limit.
Since such an overflow is highly unlikely in practice, we assume that this condition is unsat.

Note: we already assume that any sha3 hash output is smaller than 2**256 - 2**64 (see SEVM.sha3_data()).
However, the smt solver may not be able to solve this condition within the branching timeout.
In such cases, this explicit pattern serves as a fallback to avoid exploring practically infeasible paths.

We don't need to handle the negation of this condition, because unknown conditions are conservatively assumed to be sat.

functions.name: halmos.utils.stripped
documentation: Remove 0x prefix from hexstring

functions.name: halmos.utils.stringify
documentation:
Formats a value based on the inferred type of the variable.

Expects symbol_name to be of the form 'p_<sourceVar>_<sourceType>', e.g. 'p_x_uint256'

modules.name: halmos.bytevec
documentation: SPDX-License-Identifier: AGPL-3.0

functions.name: halmos.bytevec.try_concat
documentation: Attempt to concatenate two values together if they have the same type

functions.name: halmos.bytevec.defrag
documentation: Merge adjacent bytes into a single element

methods.name: halmos.bytevec.Chunk.wrap
documentation:
Wrap a value in a Chunk object to represent a span of bytes.

This is a factory method that returns either a ConcreteChunk or a SymbolicChunk.

Supported data types:
- int: a single byte, resulting in a ConcreteChunk of length 1 (raises if value does not fit in a byte)
- bytes: a sequence of bytes, resulting in a ConcreteChunk of the same length (empty bytes are supported)
- BitVecRef: if it is a bitvec value, it is converted to bytes and wrapped in a ConcreteChunk.
    Otherwise, it is wrapped in a SymbolicChunk of the same length.

methods.name: halmos.bytevec.Chunk.empty
documentation:
A convenient way to get an empty chunk.

In order to test for emptiness, instead of comparing to Chunk.empty(), it may be better to use:
- `if not chunk` or
- `if len(chunk) == 0`

methods.name: halmos.bytevec.Chunk.concretize
documentation:
Replace all symbols in the data with their corresponding concrete values,
if they exist in the given substitution mapping.

Return a new object with concrete values, or self if no substitution is made.

classes.name: halmos.bytevec.ConcreteChunk

methods.name: halmos.bytevec.ConcreteChunk.get_byte
documentation:
Return a single byte at the given offset (fast given that this is a native operation)

Complexity: O(1)

methods.name: halmos.bytevec.ConcreteChunk.slice
documentation:
Return a slice of the chunk, as a new chunk (this is fast and does not copy the backing data)

Complexity: O(1)

methods.name: halmos.bytevec.ConcreteChunk.unwrap
documentation:
Return the data as a single value, this is where the actual slicing/copying is done

Complexity: O(n)
A chunk of concrete, native bytes

classes.name: halmos.bytevec.SymbolicChunk

methods.name: halmos.bytevec.SymbolicChunk.get_byte
documentation:
Return a single byte at the given offset.

This can be slow because it involves an Extract expression on a potentially large BitVec.

Complexity: O(n)?

methods.name: halmos.bytevec.SymbolicChunk.slice
documentation:
Return a slice of the chunk, as a new chunk (this is fast and does not copy the backing data)

Complexity: O(1)

methods.name: halmos.bytevec.SymbolicChunk.unwrap
documentation:
Return the data as a single value, this is where the actual slicing/copying is done

This can be slow because it involves an Extract expression on a potentially large BitVec.

Complexity: O(n)?
A chunk of symbolic bytes

classes.name: halmos.bytevec.ChunkInfo

class_variables.name: halmos.bytevec.ChunkInfo.index
documentation: -1 if not found

class_variables.name: halmos.bytevec.ChunkInfo.end
documentation: end offset, i.e. start + len(chunk)
Metadata about a chunk at a given offset in a ByteVec.

classes.name: halmos.bytevec.ByteVec

methods.name: halmos.bytevec.ByteVec._dump
documentation: ## internal methods

methods.name: halmos.bytevec.ByteVec._load_chunk
documentation:
Locate the chunk that contains the given offset.

Complexity: O(log n) where n is the number of chunks (thanks to the backing SortedDict)

methods.name: halmos.bytevec.ByteVec.__set_chunk
documentation:
Set a chunk at the given offset.

Internal usage only, does not update length or check for overlaps.

Returns True if the chunk was set
## write operations

methods.name: halmos.bytevec.ByteVec.append
documentation:
Append a new chunk at the end of the ByteVec.

This does not copy the data, it just adds a reference to it.

Complexity: O(1)

methods.name: halmos.bytevec.ByteVec.set_slice
documentation:
Assign a byte range value to the ByteVec between offsets start (inclusive) and stop (exclusive).

Supported value types:
- bytes: a sequence of bytes
- BitVecRef: a symbolic value
- Chunk: a concrete or symbolic chunk
- ByteVec: a sequence of chunks (e.g. from reading an existing slice)

Raises if the value is not the same length as the slice.

methods.name: halmos.bytevec.ByteVec.set_word
documentation:
Write a 32-byte word at the given offset.

This is a thin wrapper that wraps the value as a Chunk and stores it.

Supported types:
- int: will be converted to 32 byte Chunk
- BitVecRef: must be 32 bytes
- bytes: must be 32 bytes
- bool: will be converted to 32 byte SymbolicChunk

methods.name: halmos.bytevec.ByteVec.concretize
documentation:
Replace all symbols in the chunks with their corresponding concrete values,
if they exist in the given substitution mapping.

Return a new ByteVec object even if no substitution is made.

methods.name: halmos.bytevec.ByteVec.slice
documentation:
Return a slice of data between offsets start (inclusive) and stop (exclusive).

If some portion of [start:stop] is out of bounds, it will be filled with zeroes.

Complexity:
- O(log(num_chunks)) to locate the first chunk
- plus complexity to iterate over the subsequent chunks (O(stop - start))
- plus complexity to slice the first and last chunks (O(1) on concrete bytes, O(n) on symbolic bytes)
- plus complexity to append the resulting chunks to a new ByteVec (O(stop - start))
## read operations

methods.name: halmos.bytevec.ByteVec.get_byte
documentation:
Return a single byte at the given offset.

If the offset is out of bounds, returns 0.

Complexity:
- O(log(num_chunks)) to locate the chunk
- plus the complexity to extract a byte (O(1) on concrete chunks, O(num_bytes) on symbolic chunks)

methods.name: halmos.bytevec.ByteVec.get_word
documentation:
Return a single word (32 bytes) at the given offset.

This is a thin wrapper that just loads a slice and converts it to a single value (int or bv) rather than bytes.

If some portion of [offset:offset+32] is out of bounds, returns zeroes.

methods.name: halmos.bytevec.ByteVec.unwrap
documentation:
Return the entire ByteVec as a single value.

This is where the actual slicing/copying is done.

Complexity: O(n)

methods.name: halmos.bytevec.ByteVec.copy
documentation:
Return a shallow copy of the ByteVec:
- copies the container for the chunks
- does not copy the chunks themselves, which are immutable

ByteVec represents a sequence of mixed concrete/symbolic chunks of bytes.

Supported operations:
- append: add a new chunk to the end of the ByteVec
- get a single byte at a given offset
- get a slice (returns a ByteVec)
- get the length of the ByteVec

- assign a byte
- assign a slice

- compare equality with another ByteVec
- unwrap (returns the entire ByteVec as a single value, either bytes or a BitVecRef)

methods.name: halmos.cheatcodes.Prank.lookup
documentation:
If `to` is an eligible prank destination, return the active prank context.

If `keep` is False, this resets the prank context.

class_variables.name: halmos.cheatcodes.Prank.active
documentation: active prank context

class_variables.name: halmos.cheatcodes.Prank.keep
documentation: start / stop prank

A mutable object to store the current prank context.

Because it's mutable, it must be copied across contexts.

Can test for the existence of an active prank with `if prank: ...`

A prank is active if either sender or origin is set.

- prank(address) sets sender
- prank(address, address) sets both sender and origin

classes.name: halmos.config.Config

methods.name: halmos.config.Config.__getattribute__
documentation:
Look up values in parent object if they are not set in the current object.

This is because we consider the current object to override its parent.

Because of this, printing a Config object will show a "flattened/resolved" view of the configuration.
## Methods

methods.name: halmos.config.Config.with_overrides
documentation:
Create a new configuration object with some fields overridden.

Use vars(namespace) to pass in the arguments from an argparse parser or
just a dictionary with the overrides (e.g. from a toml or json file).

Configuration object for halmos.

Don't instantiate this directly, since all fields have default value None. Instead, use:

 - `default_config()` to get the default configuration with the actual default values
 - `with_overrides()` to create a new configuration object with some fields overridden
TODO: add kw_only=True when we support Python>=3.10

Abstraction over contract bytecode. Can include concrete and symbolic elements.

class_variables.name: halmos.sevm.SMTQuery.assertions
documentation: list of assertion ids

classes.name: halmos.sevm.Concretization
documentation:
Mapping of terms to concrete values, and of symbols to potential candidates.

A term is mapped to a concrete value when an equality between them is introduced in the path condition.
These terms can be replaced by their concrete values during execution as needed.
Note that cyclic substitutions do not occur, as terms are reduced to a ground value rather than another term.

A symbol may also be associated with multiple concrete value candidates.
If necessary, the path can later branch for each candidate.

TODO: Currently, the branching mechanism based on candidates is only applied to calldata with dynamic parameters.
In the future, it may be used for other purposes.

class_variables.name: halmos.sevm.Path.conditions
documentation: cond -> bool (true if explicit branching conditions)

methods.name: halmos.sevm.Exec.is_panic_of
documentation:
Check if the error is Panic(k) for any k in the given error code set.
An empty set or None will match any error code.

Panic(k) is encoded as 36 bytes (4 + 32) consisting of:
    bytes4(keccak256("Panic(uint256)")) + bytes32(k)

methods.name: halmos.sevm.Exec.set_code
documentation: Sets the code at a given address.

methods.name: halmos.sevm.Exec.quick_custom_check
documentation:
Quick custom checker for specific known patterns.

This method checks for certain common conditions that can be evaluated
quickly without invoking the full SMT solver.

Returns:
    sat if the condition is satisfiable
    unsat if the condition is unsatisfiable
    None if the condition requires full SMT solving

methods.name: halmos.sevm.Exec.sha3_hash
documentation: return concrete bytes if the hash can be evaluated, otherwise None

methods.name: halmos.sevm.Exec.sha3_expr
documentation: return a symbolic sha3 expression, e.g. f_sha3_256(data)

methods.name: halmos.sevm.Exec.returndata
documentation: Return data from the last executed sub-context or the empty bytes sequence

methods.name: halmos.sevm.Exec.resolve_libs
documentation: deploy libraries and resolve library placeholders in hexcode

methods.name: halmos.sevm.SolidityStorage.init
documentation:
Initialize ex.storage[addr].mapping[slot][num_keys][size_keys], if not yet initialized
- case size_keys == 0: scalar type: initialized with zero or symbolic value
- case size_keys != 0: mapping type: initialized with empty array or symbolic array

methods.name: halmos.sevm.GenericStorage.init
documentation:
Initialize ex.storage[addr].mapping[size_keys], if not yet initialized

NOTE: unlike SolidityStorage, size_keys > 0 in GenericStorage.
      thus it is of mapping type, and initialized with empty array or symbolic array.

methods.name: halmos.sevm.SEVM.calldataload
documentation:
Handle generalized calldata encoding. (See calldata.encode for more details on generalized encoding.)

If the loaded value is a symbol, it may represent a size symbol for dynamic parameters, and can be resolved as follows:
- If the symbol is already constrained to a concrete value in the current path condition, it is replaced by that value.
- If the symbol is associated with candidate values, the current path is branched over these candidates.

methods.name: halmos.sevm.SEVM.sym_byte_of
documentation: generate symbolic BYTE opcode result using 32 nested ite
